channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: Salesforce Killed The Browser. Every Agent Runs Your CRM Now.
executive_summary: In the rapidly evolving AI landscape, companies like OpenAI, Salesforce, and Anthropic are launching new agents and tools that redefine workflows and data access. Notably, Salesforce's Headless 360 exposes over 60 new tools and APIs, making it easier for agents to interact with existing systems. This shift emphasizes the importance of infrastructure over mere features, as teams must adapt to a layered approach rather than seeking a single default solution.
bullets: Salesforce's Headless 360 exposes over 60 new MCP tools, allowing agents to access Salesforce capabilities directly through APIs. This means that agents can now operate without needing to log into the Salesforce interface, streamlining workflows significantly. Think of it like giving a delivery driver a direct route to the warehouse instead of making them navigate through the front office.

OpenAI's Workspace agents are not just custom GPTs; they are shared, cloud-based agents designed for team collaboration across tools like ChatGPT and Slack. This shift from personal assistants to reusable work units allows teams to automate repetitive tasks more efficiently. Imagine a factory where each worker has a specific role, rather than everyone trying to do every job.

Microsoft's Copilot Wave 3 integrates Anthropic's Claude technology to enhance long-running multi-step agent execution within Microsoft 365. This means that Copilot can access emails, meetings, and files, making it particularly powerful for organizations already using Microsoft products. It's like having a personal assistant who knows your entire office layout and can fetch documents without you needing to ask.

The filter for evaluating new AI agents includes five questions, starting with whether the tool integrates with existing systems. This helps eliminate products that require teams to migrate to new environments, which can be costly and disruptive. Think of it like choosing a new phone that works with your existing charger instead of needing to buy a whole new set of accessories.

Salesforce's Headless 360 allows coding agents to work with live organizational context, which means they can interact with real-time data without human intervention. This capability is crucial for automating tasks that rely on up-to-date information, like managing customer relationships. It's akin to having a mechanic who can fix your car while it's still running, rather than waiting for it to break down.

Perplexity's Personal Computer on Mac adds local file editing and browsing capabilities, enhancing its functionality as a digital worker. This allows users to interact with files directly on their machines, making it a versatile tool for research-heavy tasks. Imagine a librarian who not only knows where every book is but can also pull them off the shelf and hand them to you instantly.

The ecosystem around a product is critical; one-off launches often fade, while those with marketplaces and developer tools tend to thrive. This indicates whether a release will be sustainable in the long term. Think of it like a farmer's market that attracts a steady crowd versus a pop-up shop that disappears after a weekend.

Kimmy K 2.6, while technically impressive with its swarm architecture, does not integrate well into existing enterprise workflows. It is designed for developers who can self-host and manage their own infrastructure, making it less suitable for general business teams. It's like a high-performance sports car that requires a skilled driver; not everyone can handle it.

The question of whether to switch from one AI tool to another is now more about layering than outright replacement. Teams should consider which tool fits best for specific tasks rather than trying to find a one-size-fits-all solution. Think of it like building a toolbox; you wouldn't use a hammer for every job when you have a whole set of tools available.

Data access is crucial for agent quality; a mediocre agent with full customer history can outperform a brilliant agent with no context. This highlights the importance of integrating tools that have access to relevant data. It's like trying to solve a puzzle without all the pieces; you won't get the full picture without the right information.

Salesforce's strategy aims to become the underlying infrastructure for the agent economy, meaning that all companies using Salesforce will have access to a robust ecosystem of agents. This positions Salesforce as a central player in the future of AI-driven workflows. Imagine a city where all roads lead to a central hub, making travel easier and more efficient.

The five-question filter helps teams focus on launches that truly enhance their workflows, reducing the noise from less relevant products. This approach ensures that time and resources are spent on tools that will provide real value. It's like having a GPS that directs you only to the best routes instead of getting lost in traffic.
key_takeaways: Implement the five-question filter for evaluating new AI tools. This will help your team focus on products that integrate well with existing systems and offer real value, rather than getting distracted by flashy new features. Skipping this step could lead to wasted resources and ineffective tools.

Prioritize tools that enhance data access and workflow integration. By choosing products that connect seamlessly with your current systems, you can avoid the costly and disruptive process of migrating to new environments. If you ignore this, your team may struggle with inefficiencies and frustration.

Evaluate the specific needs of your team when considering AI tools. Different tools serve different purposes, so it's essential to match the tool to the task at hand. Failing to do this could lead to wasted licenses and ineffective workflows.

By the end of this year, focus on layering your AI tools rather than seeking a single default solution. This approach will help your team adapt to the evolving landscape of AI and ensure that you are using the best tools for each specific job.

technical_terms.Headless 360: A Salesforce initiative that exposes platform capabilities through APIs, allowing agents to access data directly without a user interface. Think of it like a restaurant kitchen where chefs can access ingredients without having to go through the dining area.
technical_terms.Workspace agents: Cloud-based agents designed for team collaboration that can work across multiple tools like ChatGPT and Slack. Like a Swiss Army knife, they provide various functions in one compact tool.
technical_terms.Copilot Wave 3: Microsoft's integration of Anthropic's Claude technology to enhance agent execution within Microsoft 365. It's like adding a turbocharger to a car, boosting its performance significantly.
technical_terms.MCP tools: Multi-Cloud Platform tools that allow for integration and interaction with various cloud services. Think of it like a universal remote that can control multiple devices at once.
technical_terms.Ecosystem: A network of products and services that work together to provide enhanced functionality. Like a symbiotic relationship in nature, where different species support each other's survival.
technical_terms.Swarm architecture: A model where multiple agents work together to complete complex tasks. It's like a flock of birds flying in formation, where each bird contributes to the group's overall success.
technical_terms.Data access: The ability of an agent to retrieve and utilize relevant information for its tasks. Think of it like a librarian having access to all the books in a library, enabling them to provide accurate information.
technical_terms.Integration: The process of connecting different systems or tools to work together seamlessly. Like a puzzle where each piece fits perfectly to create a complete picture.
technical_terms.API: Application Programming Interface, a set of rules that allows different software applications to communicate with each other. It's like a waiter taking your order and delivering it to the kitchen.
technical_terms.Digital worker: An AI system designed to perform tasks traditionally done by humans, such as research or data analysis. Think of it like a virtual assistant who can handle various tasks without needing breaks.
technical_terms.Agent economy: The growing market for AI agents that perform specific tasks within organizations. Like a bustling marketplace where different vendors offer their unique products and services.
technical_terms.Workflow: The sequence of processes through which a piece of work passes from initiation to completion. It's like a factory assembly line where each step is crucial to producing the final product.
technical_terms.Self-hosting: Running software on your own servers instead of relying on a third-party service. Think of it like owning a car rather than renting one; you have complete control over it.
technical_terms.Long-running agents: AI agents designed to perform tasks over extended periods, rather than just executing single commands. It's like a marathon runner who can sustain their pace over a long distance.
technical_terms.Open weights: A model architecture where the underlying code and parameters are publicly available for modification and use. Like an open-source recipe that anyone can tweak and improve.
technical_terms.Marketplaces: Platforms where different tools and services can be bought and sold, fostering a diverse ecosystem. Think of it like a shopping mall where various stores offer unique products.
